user_question: I enabled TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE=1 but compilation is still slow on cold start. What else can I do to reduce compile time?
issue_context:
### 🚀 The feature, motivation and pitch

`TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE=1` seems to work wonderfully in reducing inductor overhead.

However, we have yet to account for AOTAutograd and Dynamo tracing overhead.

It was found in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/113287#issuecomment-1820256862 that Dynamo and AOTAutograd overhead are comparable for inference.

On training workloads, as seen in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/113287#issuecomment-1821462237, AOTAutograd overhead is about 1.5x more than Dynamo overhead.

In particular, Dynamo and AOTAutograd tracing overhead have been found to empirically dominate when `TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE=1`, and to contribute significantly to compile overhead (~70%) in the default `TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE=0` setting.


This leads to the following insight: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/113287#issuecomment-1820151888
> If we can separate out:
> 1. the backend cacheing with `TORCHINDUCTOR_FX_GRAPH_CACHE`
> 2. the frontend cacheing with `TORCHDYNAMO_TRACING_CACHE`, which takes in some code object and serializable guards, and spits out an FX graph
>     - There is already an idea to carry this out on a smaller scale - cacheing the FX graph for inlined function calls / loop bodies https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/112303
> 3. if AOTAutograd is identified as a bottleneck, an `AOT_AUTOGRAD_CACHE`, which takes in some fx graph and returns a cached runtime wrapper, around a lazily compile...

user_question: torch.compile doesn't seem to respect torch.cuda.set_device(). My compiled function puts tensors on the wrong GPU. Is this a known issue?
issue_context:
repro:
```
@torch.compile
def f():
    a = torch.ones(2, device='cuda')
    torch.cuda.set_device(1)
    return a

print(f())
```

In eager that returns a tensor on the `cuda:0` device, while the above repro returns a tensor on `cuda:1`.

@ngimel pointed out that inductor uses the device stored on the node metadata to figure out how to set device guards in the codegen (`node.meta['val'].device)

What's probably happening is `set_device()` is not part of ATen. So when AOTAutograd traces the dynamo graph into an ATen graph, it directly calls `set_device(1)` instead of putting it into the graph. Ideally, we'd want some way of plumbing the info about the `set_device()` call into inductor

@triton.jit
def add_kernel(x_ptr,
               y_ptr,
               output_ptr,
               n_elements,
               BLOCK_SIZE: tl.constexpr):
    pid = tl.program_id(axis=0)
    block_start = pid * BLOCK_SIZE
    offsets = block_start + tl.arange(0, BLOCK_SIZE)
    mask = offsets < n_elements
    x = tl.load(x_ptr + offsets, mask=mask)
    y = tl.load(y_ptr + offsets, mask=mask)
    output = x + y
    tl.store(output_ptr + offsets, output, mask=mask)


def triton_add(x: torch.Tensor, y: torch.Tensor):
    output = torch.empty_like(x)
    n_elements = output.numel()
    grid = lambda meta: (triton.cdiv(n_elements, meta['BLOCK_SIZE']), )
    add_kernel[grid](x, y, output, n_elements, BLOCK_SIZE=1024)
    return output


def main():
    x = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda')
    y = torch.randn(4096, 4096, device='cuda')
    for _ in range(10):
        torch_add(x, y)
        torch_compile_add(x, y)
        triton_add(x, y)
        

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

user_question: Dynamo recompiles every time because it's guarding on a global config variable that I change between calls. How do I prevent guards on global state?
issue_context:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

Co-proposal with @voznesenskym and @Chillee

## Problem statement

Today, it is difficult to force a PT2 compilation with locally modified Dynamo/Inductor configuration, without having that configuration leak to other parts of the program. First, we have no convenient argument to torch.compile to locally change configuration options. This can be simulated by using torch._dynamo.config.patch. But there are still problems.

Problem 1: configuration is not guarded, so compile products of stale configs will still "hit" even if the config has changed.

```
with torch._dynamo.config.patch(CONFIG1):
    torch.compile(f)(args)  # compilation is stored on f's code object

with torch._dynamo.config.patch(CONFIG2):
    torch.compile(f)(args)  # will not recompile, CONFIG1's compile product is used
```

Problem 2: due to graph breaks, compile products may be unpredictably associated with arbitrary downstream function code objects, not just the function that was torch.compile'd

```
def g1(args): ...
def g2(args): ...
def f(args):
    g1(args)
    print("graph break")
    g2(args)

with torch._dynamo.config.patch(CONFIG1):
    torch.compile(f)(args)  # graph break means we store compile products on g1, g2

with torch._dynamo.config.patch(CONFIG2):
    torch.compile(g2)(args)  # will not recompile, CONFIG1 compile of g2 is used
```

issue_context:
The root cause was that inspect.signature was not traceable

The issue with tracing id() (mentioned in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/164247) was also a problem, but not the main one. I specialized `call_id` in BuiltinVariable to handle it for NestedUserFunctionVariable, but when I tried to patch other places in the similar way, it turned out to be an endless pit of more and more places in the code that need to be patched, related to NestedUserFunctionVariable and inspect.signature

Ultimately, with some back and forth, it led me to building a signature by hand (_signature_from_code). It's computed in NestedUserFunctionVariable.var_getattr. In SourcelessBuilder inspect Signature and Parameter are now tracked with UserDefinedObjectVariable. types.MappingProxyType is tracked too, because I kept getting "Unexpected type in sourceless builder" without it

I think this is by far the most Dynamo heavy PR I worked on and I had to experiment a lot to land the fix. I tried to limit the scope of the changes and keep to conventions in code, but probably things could be improved by someone who knows Dynamo better and I'd be very grateful for a guidance

user_question: My Triton kernel gets 1.35x slower when I add more specialization to it. Shouldn't more specialization make it faster? What's going on?
issue_context:
This triton kernel https://gist.github.com/shunting314/9bcd806bdcda7f58ab4bae57ab6f80ad (call it v1) uses ks0 as stride. If we replace ks0 with the only possible value in this test, the new kernel https://gist.github.com/shunting314/063eb3c6bf16c1b947d7608eae87381c (call it v2) get 1.35x slower.

I can verify that the slower kernel uses vectorized load/store while the faster does not. This is very counter-intuitive since vectorized load/store should help perf in general.

Actually there is an alternative change to make v2 kernel as faster as v1: we can remove in_ptr0 and out_ptr1 from the divisible_by_16 list. This by effect disables vectorized load/store as well. Here is the kernel (v3): https://gist.github.com/shunting314/827b482a0407425565051538633aa095

Overall the numbers I get seems to suggest that vectorized load/store here is bad for perf.

@bertmaher  @htyu  let me know if I should cut the issue in the triton repo instead.

UPDATE TO AVOID CONFUSION: the root cause of the slowness is not vectorization. Vectorization still help in general. The root cause is the slow kernel uses an sub-optimal layout that harm memory coalescing.
